project_summary: The project involves capturing core requirements for the CCTNS system, focusing on streamlining workflows and improving user efficiency.

propositions.text: Frequently-executed system transactions must be designed so they can be completed with a small number of interactions.
The system must avoid requiring users to click through endless screens to complete their daily work.
Page titles must be placed in a consistent location on the different pages.
The system must maintain consistency between the navigation component and the associated content whenever navigation components or overviews are shown in conjunction with associated content.
The navigation component must clearly indicate where the user is currently located.
Every single page must have a link leading to the home page of the application, or at least to a landmark page that is easy to recognize for the user.
End User and Administrator functions must be easy to use and intuitive throughout.
Text pages containing large proportions of links must be formatted so that the presence of links does not impede the readability of the text.

The system must be designed in a manner that operational data is not lost in case of any failure of equipment or communication network, and it must hold onto that information safely until the connection is back.
The user interfaces must ensure input device independence and be designed to allow activation of controls by a variety of input devices, not just a standard mouse.
The length of a page must be selected to support the primary purpose and use of the page.
The system must consult and comply with ISO 9241-303 for screen text legibility requirements.
If the visual layout is ever an issue for accessibility or preference, the user must be provided with an equivalent alternative text-only page.
A dedicated Help Module must offer detailed context-sensitive help material for all the possible actions and scenarios the user might encounter.
Context-sensitive help material must be available across all user interfaces in the application so users are never left guessing.
Text must be able to be resized by the user easily using functions provided by the user agent or other appropriate means.
Navigation links must be clearly distinguishable from controls activating some action so users shouldn't have to guess if a click will take them to a new page or trigger a process.
The system must use clear links, bulleted lists, highlighted keywords, logical headings, short phrases, and sentences to support fast skimming of text.
Horizontal scrolling must be avoided wherever possible because it really disrupts the flow.
The system must minimize the number of navigation steps needed to reach a certain piece of content.
The system must take into account different mental models, navigation strategies, and tasks of the user.
The system must offer alternative access paths for navigating to a specific unit of content.
Workspace customisation must include, but need not be limited to, changes like adjusting the menu contents.
Any space filled only with the background color must be used in such a way that it does not impair the visual skimming of the page.
The quality of textual content with respect to spelling and grammar must be sufficient so as not to impede readability.

Each presentation segment, whether a page or a window, must provide the user with a clear and sufficient indication of where they are in the navigation structure and their current segment position with respect to the overall structure.
Links that open new browser windows or pop-up windows must be clearly marked so users are not surprised.
The reading and understanding of the textual content on the screen must be supported by suitable means, such as the use of short sentences.
If a user requests access to, or searches for, a case which he does not have the right to access, the exact response needs to be selectable at configuration time.

If a user requests access to, or searches for, a case which he does not have the right to access, the system must provide a configuration option to just display title and metadata instead of the full file.
The system must allow a user to stipulate which other users or groups can access cases.
The system must allow a user to be a member of more than one group.
If frames are used in the interface, the system must provide an alternative way of presenting relevant information without frames.
Any access to cases must be stored in the audit trail.
All other activities involving the cases and related documents or data must be stored in the audit trail.
The audit trail must store any access to cases, and all other activities involving the cases and related documents or data, to assist in data recovery if something goes wrong.
The audit trail data cannot be modified in any way by any user.
The audit trail data cannot be deleted by any user.
The system must maintain the audit trail for as long as required, which means keeping it at least for the life of the case to which it refers.
The system must provide the ability to export audit trails for specified cases, but doing so must happen without affecting the audit trail stored by the System.
Any changes to access rights for groups or users must be restricted so they are made only by super-user.
Any changes to security level for groups or users must be restricted so they are made only by super-user.
Any changes to password allocation and management for groups or users must be restricted so they are made only by super-user.
The system must provide an interface for the user to log any defects on the application.
The system must provide an interface for the user to log any enhancement requests on the application.
The system must allow the user to track logged defects on the application thereafter.
The system must allow the user to track logged enhancement requests on the application thereafter.
Persistent defaults for data entry must not be limited to standard system defaults.
Persistent defaults for data entry must include user-definable values so individuals can tailor the inputs to their daily work.
Ease of use must be the main priority when mistakes happen.
All error messages produced by the system must be meaningful so they can be appropriately acted upon by the users who are likely to see them.
Whether error messages are shown on the pages themselves or on special error pages, the content must clearly state the reason why the error occurred.
Whenever possible, error messages must spell out the actions the user can take to resolve the error, rather than just giving them a dead end.
The system must allow citizens to register their complaints with the police.
The software is not expected to do all the heavy lifting on validation itself.
The police will take the complaint forward based on the evidence, facts and following investigation.
The system must support a multilingual interface.
The interfaces must be made customizable or user-configurable to the extent possible.
The setup must be compatible with specialist software that may be used.
The system must support police operations that run around the clock.
Total unplanned downtime must not exceed <xx hours/minutes> over a <rolling three month period>.
The actual number of incidents of unplanned downtime must not exceed <x> per <rolling three month period>.
In the event of any software or hardware failure, the system must be able to be restored within no more than <xx> hours.
System restoration following a software or hardware failure must be complete with inline synchronization.
The system must provide solid role-based control for the functionality within the system to manage permissions.

Whenever the platform is individualizing or adapting content based on predefined user profiles or user-specified profiles, the profile currently used must be made evident to the person looking at the screen.
Splash screens must be avoided unless they provide useful content or feedback about the application state to the user.
The entire user interface must be suitable for users with special needs.
Accessibility must be a priority for the user interface.
When designing visual cues, color must be used with care.
Color must not be used as the only means of conveying information.
The system design must take into account human capabilities and restrictions in perceiving color.
It must be made evident to the user when individualization and/or adaptation are used.
